title: Dark-Soliton Branch Blocking in Transonic Bose--Einstein Condensate Flows
abstract: Acoustic horizons in Bose--Einstein condensates are usually characterized through long-wavelength Bogoliubov phonons. We study a nonlinear counterpart: whether a one-dimensional dark-soliton branch can sustain upstream laboratory motion in a stationary transonic flow. The mechanism is local at leading order. A regular dark soliton has a bounded fluid-frame velocity, limited by the local sound speed; therefore, in the supersonic region, the background flow exceeds the largest upstream velocity available to the soliton branch. The sonic point is thus the upstream edge of the local dark-soliton branch, rather than a hard wall or a soliton geodesic surface. We construct stationary transonic Gross--Pitaevskii backgrounds and evolve the full order parameter in an open, nonperiodic domain. The simulations show upstream propagation on the subsonic side, finite-depth stalling on the subsonic side, and downstream advection for defects initialized in the supersonic region with upstream velocity relative to the fluid. Convergence, branch-consistency, local-density, phase-jump, and a dense scan of dimensionless upstream attempts support the soliton-like interpretation. The result is a branch-existence constraint, not a rigorous lower bound on arbitrary density minima of the Gross--Pitaevskii field.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the acoustic horizon in a transonic Bose-Einstein condensate acts as a barrier that prevents dark solitons from maintaining upstream motion. In the supersonic region, the background flow is too fast for the soliton branch to overcome, forcing the defects to be advected downstream. This establishes the sonic point as a fundamental limit for the existence of the dark-soliton branch.
main_problem: Investigating whether a dark-soliton branch can sustain upstream motion in a stationary transonic Bose-Einstein condensate flow and determining if the acoustic horizon acts as a branch-blocking mechanism.
main_result: The study finds that the sonic point acts as a branch-existence constraint; in the supersonic region, the background flow exceeds the maximum possible upstream velocity of the soliton, leading to downstream advection rather than upstream propagation.
method: Numerical evolution of the 1D defocusing Gross-Pitaevskii equation in an open, nonperiodic domain using a fourth-order Runge-Kutta scheme and centered finite differences.
model_or_system: A quasi-one-dimensional, repulsive, dilute Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC) in a stationary transonic flow described by the defocusing Gross-Pitaevskii equation.
key_observables: Soliton velocity (laboratory and fluid frames), density depletion (contrast), phase jump, soliton width, and Mach number.
important_parameters: Mach number (M), local sound speed (c0), dimensionless upstream velocity parameter (alpha), and the adiabatic parameter (ratio of soliton width to background length scale).
main_assumptions: The result is a branch-existence constraint rather than a rigorous lower bound on all arbitrary density minima; the local-density approximation (LDA) may weaken as the soliton approaches the sonic limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the stationary transonic background profiles; Figure 2 displays density evolutions for different flow regimes; Figures 6-8 show branch-consistency diagnostics, alpha scans, and numerical convergence; Figures 9-10 show soliton trajectories and the lack of an upstream branch in the supersonic regime; Figure 12 shows phase-jump diagnostics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonlinear branch blocking, describes the transonic BEC model and numerical methods, presents results across subsonic and supersonic regimes, validates the soliton-like nature of the defects via phase-jump and density diagnostics, and concludes with the implications of the branch-existence constraint.
why_interesting: It provides a nonlinear analogue to the acoustic horizon effect seen in phonons, demonstrating how nonlinear excitations like solitons are subject to a 'nonlinear speed-limit' in transonic flows, which is relevant for understanding defect dynamics in non-equilibrium many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Stationary transonic background used in the numer- ical simulations. The density ρ0(x) decreases from left to right, while the conservation law v0 = J/ρ0 accelerates the flow. The local sound speed is c0 = √gρ0, and the Mach number M = v0/c0 crosses unity at the acoustic horizon xH. The stationary residual is ϵR = 1.61 × 10−4.

FIG. 2. Representative density evolutions |ψ(x, t)|2 for the three dynamical regimes. The solid curve in each panel is the extracted trajectory Xs(t), and the dashed vertical line marks the acoustic horizon xH. (a) Subsonic upstream propagation for X0 = 20 and u0 = −0.75: the soliton remains on the subsonic side and moves toward smaller x. (b) Finite-depth stalling on the subsonic side for X0 = 20 and u0 = −0.60: the soliton is initially directed upstream in the local fluid frame but drifts slowly downstream because its relative velocity is insufficient to overcome the local convective flow. (c) Super- sonic downstream advection for X0 = 70 and u0 = −0.60: even though the initial velocity...

FIG. 3. Tracked soliton trajectories for the three repre- sentative regimes. In the subsonic upstream case, a suffi- ciently large negative relative velocity produces motion to- ward smaller x. A finite-depth subsonic attempt is nearly stalled and then drifts downstream. In the supersonic region the trajectory is strictly downstream, illustrating the absence of a sustained upstream dark-soliton branch when v0 > c0.

FIG. 4. Velocity-branch diagnostics for the three representa- tive regimes. The solid curve in each panel is the smoothed soliton velocity ˙Xs(t) extracted from the tracked density min- imum, while the dashed curve is the local adiabatic branch edge v0[Xs(t)] −c0[Xs(t)]. (a) In the subsonic upstream- propagation case, the branch edge is negative, and the soliton can realize upstream laboratory motion. (b) In the finite- depth stalling case, the branch edge remains negative, but the finite-depth soliton drifts weakly downstream and stays close to the separatrix. (c) In the supersonic downstream- advection case, the branch edge is positive, and the extracted soliton velocity remains positive.

FIG. 5. Extracted soliton depth for the three representative regimes. The depth evolves slowly due to background inho- mogeneity and weak radiation. The tracked density depletion remains a coherent, soliton-like object during the interval used to test the branch criterion while exhibiting the expected de- formation in the inhomogeneous flow.
